goal: Step 5 v1: agent eval harness — fixture format, embedded fixtures, RetrieveContext-based precondition scorer

summary.title: Agent-eval harness v1: fixture catalog + precondition scorer
summary.what: New `internal/eval` package implementing Step 5 from spec v0.2 — Fixture / SeedIntent / ExpectedItem / ScoreFixture types, BuildView (pure: fixture → synthetic MainlineView), an embedded catalog of 8 fixtures (4 populated covering the four retrieval-status branches; 4 stubs with [stub] markers), Runner with a Retriever interface so the substrate stays import-free of engine, and a CLI surface (`mainline eval list` / `mainline eval run [name]`) that wires the real engine into a scratch repo per fixture. Tests: 8 substrate-layer units plus the live run on the dogfood repo (4/4 pass).
summary.why: Spec §11 says the validation experiment is the load-bearing milestone — without it, the product thesis has not been tested. The full validation needs an LLM runner that drives both a code-first and an intent-first agent across the same fixtures and compares outcomes; that is a substantial follow-up. v1 ships the substrate and the precondition scorer, which answers a strictly-weaker but still load-bearing question: does retrieval surface the constraints at all? A constraint retrieval cannot surface is one no agent can respect regardless of prompt. So this PR proves the precondition holds for the four most representative scenarios in the spec list of 8.
summary.user_goal: Have a runnable harness that lets us start populating fixtures without waiting for the LLM runner, and that proves retrieval is doing its job before we measure agent behaviour on top.

decisions.chose: Substrate + 4 populated fixtures + 4 stubs + precondition scorer. No LLM runner.
decisions.rationale: LLM runner needs API keys, async control, prompt iteration, and result parsing — its own design exercise. Splitting it off keeps this PR focused on the deterministic substrate that the LLM layer will sit on top of.

decisions.chose: internal/eval depends only on internal/domain; engine wire-up lives in internal/cli/eval.go.
decisions.rationale: Pure-data substrate stays cheap to import from tests, future LLM runners, and external tooling. Engine pulls in storage / git / a lot of surface; we don't want eval to inherit those just because the CLI happens to use them.

decisions.point: synthetic view vs real seal flow
decisions.chose: BuildView synthesises a *domain.MainlineView and writes it directly via Store.WriteMainlineView.
decisions.rationale: RetrieveContext only reads the materialised view; running the seal/commit/sync pipeline per fixture would be 10× slower without testing anything new. Same trade-off the new PBT tests made.

decisions.chose: Fixtures with len(Intents)==0 are skipped (not failed).
decisions.rationale: Lets the catalog carry the spec's full 8-fixture list from day one without false-failing. Skipped count keeps AllPassed=false until they're filled in or removed; that's an honest 'we have not finished yet' signal.

decisions.chose: Per-Expected-item pass/fail with substring-match anti_patterns, exact-match status, and a free-form Note.
decisions.rationale: Fixture authors don't have to know exact wording an agent recorded; they pin the constraint by substring. Status uses exact match because the four retrieval-status values are a closed set.

rejected.alternative: Ship the LLM runner in this PR.
rejected.reason: Two design exercises in one. The substrate's quality is what makes the LLM runner cheap; bundling them blurs the contract.

rejected.alternative: Score by running agents and parsing their git diffs.
rejected.reason: Out of scope for v1; that's exactly the LLM runner work. The precondition scorer is a strictly-cheaper but still load-bearing question.

rejected.alternative: Make ScoreFixture interpret the Forbidden list as automatic checks.
rejected.reason: Forbidden lists are descriptive — they will be matched against agent output by the LLM runner once it lands. Pretending the substrate can score them today would invite false-positive 'fixture passes' that aren't actually testing the forbidden behaviour.

Go structs are typechecked, refactor-friendly, and live next to the harness. YAML would just be untyped versions of the same data with a parser to maintain. The cost was zero today; the cost in a year would be non-zero.

risks: The fixtures encode a particular reading of spec §9. If those readings drift from what the LLM runner actually needs, fixtures may need rewriting when the runner lands. Acceptable: the substrate is small (one Fixture struct) and pure.

Substring matching on AntiPattern.What is forgiving but not perfect — a fixture that wants 'OAuth' might match 'oauth-discovery' even when the constraint is about something else. v1: lower-risk to be forgiving; tighten only if a false-positive shows up.

Skipped-stub fixtures keep AllPassed=false. CI-wise this means `mainline eval run` always exits non-zero today. That's deliberate — the catalog is honestly incomplete — but it does mean don't wire `eval run` into a green-checkmark CI gate before either populating the stubs or filtering them out.

anti_patterns.why: Substrate stays pure; engine wire-up is the CLI's responsibility. Inverting this couples eval to git/storage and makes the harness expensive to use from tests or future LLM runners.

anti_patterns.what: Treating ScoreFixture's Forbidden list as automatic checks
anti_patterns.why: Forbidden is descriptive — it documents what the LLM runner will check, not what the substrate scores. Auto-checking it would silently pass fixtures that aren't actually testing the agent's compliance.

anti_patterns.what: Adding a fixture without the [stub] marker but leaving Intents empty
anti_patterns.why: RunAll skips empty-Intents fixtures; without the marker the catalog test fails so reviewers notice the gap. Removing the marker without seeding Intents breaks the contract test.

followups: LLM runner: drive Anthropic / OpenAI API with both code-first and intent-first prompts, parse agent output, score against Forbidden lists.
Populate the four stub fixtures (billing-boundary, risk-aware-tests, docs-only-intent, refactor-cross-file). Each needs ground-truth setup + Expected list + Forbidden list.
Score `mainline eval run --json` output to a markdown report so eval results land in PR descriptions automatically.
Spec §5.5 (Publish Eval Results): once the LLM runner ships and produces a code-first vs intent-first delta, write the eval-results blog and open-source the fixture catalog separately.
Add per-fixture seed for randomness (the harness is deterministic today; if it stops being so when the LLM runner lands, seeded reproduction will matter).

architectural_claims: internal/eval is pure data + scoring logic; engine wire-up belongs in the CLI layer.
Fixtures are Go structs in-package, not YAML files: typechecked, refactor-friendly, and they live next to the harness.
v1 scores the precondition (retrieval surfaces the constraint?), not the agent (does the agent act on it?). The latter requires an LLM runner and is a separate layer.
behavioral_changes: New CLI subtree: `mainline eval list` and `mainline eval run [name]`. Exits 2 on any failure.
Embedded catalog of 8 fixtures (4 populated, 4 stubs).

api_changes.signature: eval.Retriever interface { RetrieveByQuery(query string, limit int) ([]Retrieved, error) }
